data.title: Liberty Park Capital Q1 2026 Letter To Partners
data.description:
Dear Partner,

Liberty Park Fund, LP's value decreased by 5.38%, net of fees, in the first quarter of 2026 vs. a 0.89% increase in the Russell 2000. Our long positions contributed 1.55%, while our shorts detracted 7.91%. Gross exposure averaged 177.16%. Net exposure averaged 32.72%. Gross Pure Alpha 1 — our proprietary measure of returns generated from stock selection — was -3.12% for the quarter.

Liberty Park Select Opportunities, LP's value decreased by 5.50% net of fees in the third quarter. Gross exposure averaged 90.00%.

Liberty Park Fund 1 Liberty Park Select Opportunities 2 Benchmark Returns
Average Net Long Exposure Net Return Net Return Russell 2000
1Q26 32.72% -5.38% -5.50% 0.89%
Trailing 12 Months 34.39% -10.12% 20.34% 25.72%
Annualized Since Inception 21.42% 4.74% 11.37%
**Please see final page for disclaimers**

1Q26 Performance Analysis

Over the past year, markets have been especially difficult for us to navigate. Retail investors following social media, LLM-powered stock pickers, and passive index flows are all funneling capital into the same handful of names. That crowding creates a feedback loop where the more money that flows in, the better the performance, which attracts more money. Fundamental analysis doesn't have much of a role in that feedback loop.

We've been on the wrong side of it, but we also don't think the relative underperformance is permanent. Crowding of this magnitude has historically unwound violently.

Long Performance

Neogen Corp (NEOG) NEOG 32.90% 1.25% N/A

  • AESI shares rallied during the quarter with oil prices as the conflict with Iran brought Strait of Hormuz traffic to a standstill.
  • NEOG shares rose after the first earnings with new management showed significant progress on improving margins and deleveraging.
  • KRT earnings showed sales accelerated from record levels and guidance indicated that margins should improve as pricing changes and supply chain diversification begin to offset the impact from tariffs.
Worst Performing Longs
Name Ticker Return LPF Contribution Select Contribution
Xometry Inc (XMTR) XMTR -31.33% -1.29% -3.15%
Latham Group Inc (SWIM) SWIM -15.43% -0.72% -0.81%
Ascent Industries Co (ACNT) ACNT -17.79% -0.68% N/A
  • XMTR reported its best quarter as a public company demonstrating accelerating revenue growth and margin expansion. However, the company reported earnings the day after a widely-circulated Substack detailed a doomsday scenario that questioned if AI agents weaken the durability of network effects.
  • SWIM reported better-than-expected fourth quarter results and issued guidance above expectations. The war in Iran, however, has increased raw material prices and interest rates, sending SWIM shares lower.
  • ACNT gave back recent gains after reporting results for its seasonally weak fourth quarter.

Short Performance and Outlook

When a hole in the ground costs $1 to dig but is priced in the stock market at $10, the temptation to reach for a shovel becomes irresistible. - Edward Chancellor

The current AI infrastructure wave represents a supply-side explosion where the competitive urge of hyperscalers to not fall behind one another has decoupled capital expenditure from historical return-on-investment norms. In other words, we are watching a game of Russian roulette.

Google (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), and Oracle (ORCL) project combined 2026 capex of greater than $600 billion — a figure that is materially higher than their combined operating profits expected this year. Unlike undersea cables or land, GPU clusters carry a short useful life, creating a massive, looming depreciation drag on future income statements. On top of the hyperscalers' investments, venture-backed "neoclouds" and private credit funds are adding a secondary layer of supply, putting further downward pressure on the future marginal cost of compute.

To avoid a structural collapse in capital returns, the industry requires profit outcomes equivalent to building multiple new Google-size businesses from scratch. In fact, to sustain a 30%+ return on invested capital, the industry needs an incremental $1.5T-$2T in annual AI-driven revenue by 2027 at 30% profit margins.

If intelligence becomes a commodity, its price will collapse toward the cost of the energy used to produce it. Utilities, however, typically operate at mid-teens EBITDA margins, not the 70% margins of scaled software businesses.

Where will the revenue come from? We have all heard stories of how AI is eliminating days', weeks' and even months' worth of work that multiple humans did before, and it is doing it for almost nothing. Presumably, commercial AI companies like Anthropic will eventually start to raise prices, but right now, they are focused on gaining users and market share, and they are spending much, much more than they are charging. That can't last forever.

We expect the industry faces the same reckoning that has followed every prior infrastructure supercycle. Potential catalysts for a re-rating are not hard to identify: OpenAI missing its own revenue targets, multiple IPOs hitting the market in a short period of time and flipping the trend from share repurchases to share issuances, or a sudden withdrawal of Gulf sovereign capital should regional conflict interrupt the flow of money underwriting much of the buildout.

In Full Disclosure

Liberty Park Partners, LP (our GP) will redeem less than $30,000 from Liberty Park Select Opportunities, LP on April 30, 2026 to facilitate the payment of employees' annual bonuses.

Charles P. Murphy, CFA, Managing Partner, Portfolio Manager

Kurt A. Probe, CFA, Partner, Co-Portfolio Manager, Director of Research

Andrew Wang, Partner, Co-Portfolio Manager, Analyst

data.title: Consumer Confidence Edged Up Again Despite Spiking Prices
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index® edged up for the third consecutive month in April, rising 0.6 points to 92.8. While the recent monthly gains have been modest, the index has reached its highest level of the year and outpaced the forecast of 89.0. Note that the survey period for this month's results was April 1st-22nd, a timeframe that included the temporary two ceasefires in the Middle East and the subsequent rebound in U.S. equities.

The Present Situation Index, which is based on consumers' assessment of current business and labor market conditions, fell 0.3 points to 123.8. Meanwhile, the Expectations Index, which is based on consumers' short-term outlook for income, business, and labor market conditions, rose 1.2 points to 72.2. Note that a level of 80 or below for the Expectations Index historically signals a recession within the next year, and the index has been below 80 since February 2025.

“Consumer confidence edged up in April but was overall little changed, despite material concern about rising gasoline prices as the war in the Middle East prompted a surge in Brent crude oil prices,” said Dana M Peterson, Chief Economist, The Conference Board. “Consumer appraisals of current and expected business conditions declined moderately compared to last month. This was offset by modest improvements in consumers’ perceptions of the labor market, both current and expected, as well as income expectations, which were slightly more optimistic in April.”

Consumers’ write-in responses on factors affecting the economy continued to skew towards pessimism in April. Comments about prices, oil and gas, and war increased in frequency compared to March—a likely signal of consumers’ underlying worries about how the war in the Middle East will impact their pockets.

A two-week ceasefire and a rebound in stock market indices within the survey-sample period (April 1–22) likely helped ease concerns about financial indicators somewhat in April after spiking in March. Still, consumers remained warry. Consumers’ average and median 12-month inflation expectations ticked downward but continued to be elevated. The percentage of consumers saying interest rates over the next 12 months will be higher on net rose to nearly 50%. Expectations for higher stock prices a year from now ticked up.

Read more

Background on the Consumer Confidence Index

The Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index measures the consumers' attitudes and confidence in the economy, business conditions, and labor market, with higher readings indicating higher optimism. The general assumption is that when consumers are more optimistic, they will spend more and stimulate economic growth.

However, if consumers are pessimistic, then spending will decline and the economy may slow down. The index is based on a 5-question survey, with 2 questions related to present conditions and 3 questions related to future expectations. The survey began in 1967 and was conducted every two months but changed to monthly reporting in 1977, which is where our data begins.

The next two charts are attempts to evaluate the historical context for this index as a coincident indicator of the economy. In this chart, I have highlighted recessions and the level the index was at at the start of each recession. The average of the consumer confidence index at the start of recessions is 101.9, a level we have been below since February 2025.

In this next chart I have included an overlay with the GDP. It is easy to see the dips in consumer confidence when GDP is negative.

The next chart includes regression lines through the consumer confidence index and through the GDP series. Interestingly, the GDP regression has a slightly negative slope, while consumer confidence has been increasing over the same time frame (since the late 1970s). It's clear that consumer confidence begins falling shortly before official recession calls.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, let's take a look at the correlation between consumer confidence and small business sentiment, the latter by way of the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index. The consumer measure is the more volatile of the two, so it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

As the chart illustrates, the two have tracked one another fairly closely since the onset of the financial crisis. The two have diverged at brief periods and been highly correlated at others.

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

data.title: Hang Lung Group: International Shifts Seem Constructive For Investment
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund (TAREX) Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter.

Insights from the Emerging Trends in Real Estate report were not exclusive to the U.S., however. In fact, some of the more notable shifts internationally included (i) Asia Pacific's "retail scene" offering "strong opportunities" and (ii) additional progress on the regulatory front further solidifying China's real estate investment trust ("REIT") market. Both of which seem constructive for the Fund's recent investment in Hang Lung Group (HNLGY).

Founded in 1960, Hang Lung Group is a well-capitalized Hong Kong-based real estate enterprise that owns approximately 2.9 million square feet of highly leased retail and office properties in Hong Kong and Shanghai with a near net-cash position. Importantly, the company also owns a 65.1% interest in Hang Lung Properties (HLPPY), a separately listed real estate operating company with an additional 29 million square feet of retail centric properties in Hong Kong and Mainland China, as well as 10 million square feet of developments and residential projects available-for-sale.

In combination, Hang Lung Group is viewed to control one of the most productive (and valuable) retail-centric portfolios in the Asia Pacific region, with some of its iconic properties including Plaza 66 in Shanghai and the Fashion Walk in Causeway Bay. The path to establishing this platform has been far from easy though. As a matter of fact, Hang Lung Properties has spent the better part of three decades building out the core portfolio of its 11 market-dominant mixed-use locations, with more than $15 billion of "capex" in Hong Kong Dollars this decade alone. At one point, Hang Lung Properties even had to reduce its dividend, with Hang Lung Group electing to take shares instead of cash for further support.

Due to such strong sponsorship, Hang Lung Properties can now embark on a more operational focused strategy under Chair Adriel Chan at an opportune time. For instance, Hang Lung Properties is delivering its final ground-up mixed-use development this year (i.e., Westlake 66 in Hangzhou) at the same time prime retail locations seem to be benefitting from a rebound in retail sales, as well as a structural focus on further boosting consumption in the region. The supply-and-demand dynamics for the residential markets have also improved in Hong Kong and tier-1 Chinese cities more recently.

Therefore, it is not difficult to envision a path whereby Hang Lung Properties further improves its financial position beside residential sales, while reinstating a higher dividend alongside improved cash flows. Given the changes on the regulatory front, the company could also explore placing a portion of its highly recognized "66"-branded properties into a REIT vehicle to surface additional value over time.

Such actions would likely result in a material improvement to Hang Lung Properties' cost of capital, with the stock currently trading at less than 40% of book value. That said, these developments would have a more significant impact for Hang Lung Group, in our view, when considering its stock trades at roughly 20% of book value, as well as more than 50% below the market value of its stake in Hang Lung Properties (without even factoring in the directly owned assets). Such a setup is referred to as a "double discount" at Third Avenue, and one that can be quite rewarding, in our experience.

data.title: PFFR: An 8% Yield With Incremental Interest Rate Optionality
data.description:
InfraCap REIT Preferred ETF (PFFR) is a preferred shares ETF focusing on replicating the Indxx REIT Preferred Stock Index. As a result, the fund is passive in nature. The 30-day SEC yield at present is ~7.94% which is decent, along with a ~8.3% distribution yield based on recent trailing distribution. The fund charges expense ratio of ~0.45%, which is rather moderate and not altogether on a lower side, but isn’t excessive either, considering the nature of the ETF to be that of investing in preferred securities. Considering that the ETF is primarily exposed to REITs, a look into the underlying security composition and performance is warranted, along with certain important additional aspects for assessing whether the ETF holds a place in the portfolio of income-seeking investors.

Performance Evaluation and Interest Rates

Over the last five years, PFFR has not had a satisfactory performance. The ETF has provided a rather nominal return of ~1.3% on an annualized basis for the recent five years. The reason for this is the decline in the market price of the ETF. That decline, however, was not primarily the result of any fundamental issues with the underlying holdings but has largely stemmed from the rise in interest rates leading to the higher required rate of return and compressing the market prices of fixed income asset classes. The underlying REITs and its preferred securities by nature are long duration assets and therefore exhibit a fair amount of sensitivity to interest rates, particularly with the rising of the interest rates.

At the same time however, the dynamics are seemingly different now from what they were a five years ago. At present, interest rates are at a ten-year historically elevated levels. A ~4.36% market rate is different from a ~1.5%-1.75% levels which happened to be the case around five years prior. The expectations if any at presently are more towards the declining of the market rates from current range on a more structural basis.

If and when interest rate cycles reverses from the current rising trend to a potential decline, a significant mean reversion would tend to occur in funds like PFFR which would not only cover their lost ground due to market price appreciation, but would offer a compelling proposition for investors allocating to the vehicle at present. The tenure of current Fed Chair Jerome Powell stands to expire on May 15, 2026. It would be interesting to watch whether Powell might continue to remain on as a member of the Federal Reserve Board or step down as is typically the case. There is little doubt that under his leadership, interest rates saw a significant upcycle. While that might have been a good response to excessive liquidity in the markets, the said cycle has resulted in fixed income investors who would typically be invested in long-duration assets to lose significantly both in absolute terms and as an opportunity cost. While most other asset classes have performed well over the recent time period, fixed income remains the one which has taken a significant drawdown.

While it would still not be prudent to signal a possible stance on interest rates on a near or even medium term basis with any conclusive opinion, while also factoring in increasing uncertainty in macro aspects, a point on a structural basis can still be made that the probability of further hikes, at least from the current levels, to an extent we saw in the last five years is seemingly lower, while the probability of rate cuts, even on a delayed basis, appears higher. In the case that interest rates remain the same, the investor therefore would still be getting a decent yield; if they decline, a meaningful capital appreciation would get added onto the total returns. The interest rate trajectory therefore results in an asymmetric risk-reward proposition.

There is an important caveat, however, that this is only the case so far that the underlying preferred securities of the ETF are trading at a discount to par, largely due to previous price declines. The upside is not fully uncapped and this is therefore not a vehicle for a pure-play bet on interest rates. The key factor remains the current distribution yield and any capital appreciation expectation should only be considered as a secondary incremental factor

Portfolio Allocation

Discussing interest rates is always important while analysing a fixed income portfolio. In the end they are the ones directly impacting the required rate of return and thereby the market pricing of these securities. However, it is still only around half the work done. Unless we are sure of the underlying credit profile of the proposition, the allocation, however favorable with respect to the macro environment appears, is not fully justified.

While I won't be worrying about exposure to any particular underlying security, due to the diversified nature of the ETF at least on the security level, when we look into sector allocation, ~74% of the portfolio is allocated towards property REITs. These do tend to get influenced by factors impacting real estate values, rental yields and related aspects. The high exposure of the fund exposes the investors to sector-specific risk. In addition, a majority of securities are unrated. While this fact in itself is not always an issue, it is nevertheless suggestive of the need for increased caution and careful monitoring.

The methodology of security selection is further primarily rule-based, with not much emphasis given on underlying quality. I don’t think this method of selection would give investors any comfort in terms of market distress. Also, since the ETF is a passive tracker, it has to largely allocate as per the underlying allocations of the index. This again exposes an investor to market risk which, in active allocation, might to an extent be manageable through allocation changes.

That said however, considering the nature of the underlying securities to be in the form of preferred shares, which have a different risk profile than the common units of these REITs, provides some comfort. In addition, a long track record of consistent dividend distributions strengthens the case as well. While the distributions didn’t see any growth over time, in current times, due to the fall in market price of the ETF, the same amount of distribution results in a higher yield to investors on current market price. An over 358-basis point yield over a risk-free rate, for a preferred share dividend ETF, doesn’t look altogether lacking. The same yield is roughly provided by the underlying securities of the ETF. The SEC yield at present is at ~7.94%, which when compared to a ~4.36% 10-year Treasury rate is higher by 3.58%. While this still isn’t a risk-free proposition, which is already reflected by the higher yield, at least the ETF is not concentrated at the issuer level, even if sectoral concentration is there, suggesting some comfort to an extent. However, the ETF would only find a place as an addition to other fixed income products as per an investor’s overall asset allocation.

Equity REITs Relative to Broader Equities

In addition, REITs as an asset class are trading at a relatively more appealing valuation trajectory as compared with the broader S&P 500. The below chart from Nareit reflects the point on an overall basis, with the ratio of S&P 500 earnings multiples to REIT FFO (earnings equivalent) at a higher level, both on an absolute basis and as well as over a historical 20-year period excluding the period of ~2007–2008. Even though this is an analysis of preferred shares, any increase in the attractiveness of common equity does tend to get passed on to the underlying preferred stocks, to a certain extent.

Additional Comment on Interest Rate and Relation with PFFR ETF

An assertion was made earlier that a decline in interest rates would tend to benefit the ETF. While the reasoning is correct, a point which is important to note is that the possible price appreciation would largely be limited to the extent of the previous prices we saw for this fund at the time of the rate hike cycle. In other words, this would be more of a rebound from previous price declines rather than a standalone appreciation. The reason for this is that while many underlying preferred securities are perpetual in nature and exhibit greater interest rate sensitivity, the convexity often happens to turn negative on the upside as the price approaches par. This has largely to do with the call rights the issuer of these securities has, by which they can redeem the securities at par. This is particularly done when the issuer is well capitalised and a lower cost refinancing is available in the market. As the price moves closer to par, the embedded call option held by the issuer becomes more valuable, and the price stops responding to rate declines. Since the ETF is composed of underlying preferred securities, many of which do have these call rights, the limitation of convexity on the upside needs to be kept in mind. This does not impact the regular income return for the fund but is important for investors to be aware of.

With reference to the interest rate outlook, an expectation of structural decline remains intact, although with near-term possible uncertainty. However, importantly, the case is largely between rates declining versus them remaining at present levels. The probability of moving further up is relatively low on a long-term basis. The below graph from the Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) from the FOMC reflects this expectation well. This aspect tends to make the case in general stronger, particularly for long-duration assets, including REITs. In the current ETF, we already saw a price decline caused due to the rise in interest rates. Therefore, on an overall basis, a reversal of prior rate hikes might also possibly lead to a reversal of previous price declines to a meaningful extent, which can result in increased total gains in addition to a decent ~8% distribution yield offered at the current prices.

Risk Aspects

PFFR is inherently concentrated in property-based REITs, which in case of any market-wide real estate downcycle would negatively impact securities collectively, making the correlation particularly high. The market and sector risk can make underlying securities vulnerable. The liquidity of many of those securities is thin, which can exaggerate downsides. The fund, being passive in nature, would tend to track the benchmark irrespective of the underlying economic trajectories and cannot change the allocation on a tactical basis.

In addition, there is no guarantee that the underlying securities would witness a price appreciation in case of rising interest rates. The peculiarities of underlying security-specific terms and features can negate the positive impact of falling interest rates, in addition to the fact that any interest rate decline caused as a response to a market crash/correction would also not lead to an increase in prices. In times of market disruptions, credit spreads often rise due to multiple reasons, including increased default probabilities, which can even lead to a decline in security prices even in times of rate declines.

A ~7.94% SEC yield, which is 3.58% above the risk-free rate, is necessarily a result of underlying exposure to the risk inherent in the allocation. This is therefore better considered as an incremental addition, if any, to the portfolio at large along with other fixed income vehicles. Any overemphasis on high yield can lead to possible disappointment.

In Conclusion

At present market price of ~$17.8, with a distribution yield of ~8.3% funded primarily by an underlying SEC yield of ~7.94%, appears to offer adequate return with reference to the risk exposure accepted. An incremental upside with the possibility of a decline in interest rates increases the appeal. However, the proposition does not warrant any overallocation and should be analyzed carefully with respect to overall portfolio allocation of an investor. PFFR is rated a buy, but after acknowledging the risks inherent in the proposition. The yield although justified, but at the same time does not appear mispriced either.

data.title: Brookdale Senior Living: A Compelling Backdrop
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Third Avenue Real Estate Value Fund Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter.

The Urban Land Institute and PwC collaborate each year to produce Emerging Trends in Real Estate, a widely followed report that synthesizes key insights across the property sector. The most recent edition included viewpoints from a wide range of senior professionals and proved to be rich in perspective, with the following themes standing out to Fund Management in particular:

  1. Durable interest in real estate from institutional investors, with target allocations remaining above 10.5%. Despite a slight decline, real estate remains a core pillar with some noting the asset class as "looking relatively attractive against other investments as a new cycle gathers momentum."
  2. A further acceptance of property types that were once considered "niche", including operational sectors such as self-storage, logistics, and senior living that are "now widely seen as capable of delivering higher, more stable yields amid compressing traditional returns".
  3. A greater focus on investing in platforms versus individual assets, due to a more elevated cost of capital in the sector "shifting real estate returns towards operational performance and income growth rather than valuation gains".

In combination, these concepts seem to support Third Avenue's long-held focus on well-established real estate enterprises. They also seem to offer a compelling backdrop for the Fund's recent investment in the common stock of Brookdale Senior Living (BKD) ("Brookdale").

Founded in 1978, Brookdale is a leading owner and operator of senior housing properties with nearly 600 communities across the U.S. serving more than 50,000 residents. While the portfolio is vast, the company has a particular focus on assisted-living and memory-care properties, which sector specialists would point out as a more resilient segment with "needs based" demand and private-pay residents. Those same professionals would also likely perceive Brookdale as one of the premier operators in the assisted-living segment.

That said, the company has endured a difficult ten-year stretch. In fact, the Fund was previously invested in Brookdale but elected to move on after the previous management team made a somewhat hasty acquisition (i.e., Emeritus) at the same time the sector was facing elevated levels of supply. In combination with the headwinds the senior housing sector encountered amid the pandemic, Brookdale has generally been on the "defensive" in recent years shedding Emeritus locations, restructuring debt and associated leases, and attempting to stabilize its core portfolio.

While undoubtedly a challenging period, Fund Management believes the next five years will be transformative. To wit, Brookdale has solidified its financial position by returning to profitability and reducing its loan-to-value ratio below 40%, with the remaining debt largely comprised of non-recourse mortgage debt. The company has also improved its operational structure by adding a well-regarded CEO (with previous experience at industry peer Sunrise Living) and adopting a more decentralized framework. In addition, Brookdale has tremendous growth prospects with the opportunity to significantly increase its 82.1% occupancy rate alongside hugely supportive demand trends (i.e., the 80+ year-old demographic is expected to increase by 55% in the U.S. over the next decade).

As a result, Brookdale is positioned to close the performance gap with industry peers, in our view, as well as approach a 95.0% occupancy rate over time. Should such a path materialize, the company has the potential to generate more than $3.5 billion of annual revenues. By our estimates, a disproportionate share of that incremental cash flow would fall to the bottom line given the inherent operating leverage in senior housing (i.e., high fixed costs) and $2.0 billion of tax loss carry forwards.

Therefore, it would not be inconceivable for Brookdale to utilize retained earnings to further reduce its debt levels and reinstate an annual dividend. The company could also engage in additional "bolt on" acquisitions to expand its platform within core markets for the rapidly growing senior population. That said, such a scenario does not seem to be "priced in" with Brookdale trading at a discount to its owned real estate in our estimation-without factoring in any value for its operating platform, third party management business, or deferred tax assets.
