data.title: Bright Horizons Family Solutions: Strong Execution, But Valuation Keeps It A 'Hold'
data.description:
One very interesting company that I came across not long ago but have not analyzed up until this point is Bright Horizons Family Solutions Inc. (BFAM). Over the last few years now, the business, which operates as a provider of high-quality early education and childcare services, not to mention backup care solutions and educational advisory services, has experienced some nice top-line and bottom-line growth. This has come even as it has cut back on the number of locations that it has in operation to optimize its financial footprint. This overall trajectory has been positive, and management expects that trend to continue throughout at least 2026.

I would say that the shares of the company are not very cheap. But they are priced at a level that teeters between being undervalued and fairly valued. Relative to other similar enterprises, the stock is in the same boat. So at the end of the day, out of an abundance of caution, I believe that taking the more cautious approach with a "Hold" rating makes sense. But it really wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it from here.

I hope that an upgrade can come as soon as May 5. Although management has not yet announced the exact day that earnings will come out, last year, on May 5, results were announced for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. So in all likelihood, this year will be very similar. The current expectation is that revenue and adjusted profitability will rise. And if things go incredibly well and we see an upward revision in guidance, upgrading the stock to a soft "Buy" would not be unfathomable. For now, however, the more cautious approach makes more sense to me.

Learning About Bright Horizons Family Solutions

As I mentioned already, Bright Horizons Family Solutions operates as a provider of high-quality early education and childcare, as well as comprehensive backup care solutions and educational advisory services. The firm has been in operation for 40 years now. And in that time, it has grown into a global enterprise. According to management, the company has 1,010 locations in operation, with only 597 of those here in the United States. 413 of them are located in overseas markets, specifically the UK, the Netherlands, Australia, and India. The company serves more than 1,450 employers, including no fewer than 220 of the Fortune 500 companies. They do this under multi-year contracts with employer-clients who offer early education and childcare, backup care, and educational advisory services as part of their employee benefits packages.

71% of the company's revenue last year came from its full-service center-based childcare activities. About 75% of these facilities, all of which provide childcare at centers located at or near an employer-sponsored worksite, operate using a profit and loss model. In this scenario, the company retains the financial outcomes of operating the center in question. But then the other 25% of these centers operate under a cost-plus business model. In essence, it receives a fee from the employer sponsor for managing and operating the center in question.

Over the last three years now, the company has achieved some pretty impressive growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue expanded from $2.42 billion to $2.93 billion. A lot of this increase in revenue can be chalked up to higher tuition rates that it has charged in exchange for its services. In fact, it's likely that revenue would have fallen otherwise. I say this because, as the charts above and below illustrate, both the number of children serviced and the number of locations that the business has in operation have dropped over the last few years. By continuing to focus on optimizing the best-performing assets, including boosting occupancy at existing ones that it decides to keep, the firm believes that it can capture more value for investors in the long run. That's why management is continuing to focus on closing underperforming locations. This year, for instance, it is planning to close between 45 and 50 such centers.

The rate increases that the company has achieved, combined with other operational changes that the company implemented to boost occupancy, clearly have had a big impact on the bottom line. Net income jumped from $74.2 million in 2023 to $193.1 million in 2025. Adjusted net profits grew from $164.3 million to $261.5 million. Other profitability metrics improved also. Take operating cash flow as an example. According to the company, this jumped from $256.1 million to $350.7 million over the last three years. And if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $250.4 million to $365.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $352.1 million to $487.4 million.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects the rationalization activities and price hikes that it is implementing to push revenue up even more to between $3.075 billion and $3.125 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a year-over-year increase of 5.7%. As revenue increases, profitability should rise also. The current plan is for adjusted earnings per share of between $4.90 and $5.10. Hitting the midpoint of that range would give us an adjusted net income of $287.1 million. And if we scale up other profitability metrics similarly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $401.3 million and EBITDA of $535.1 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the results the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. These certainly aren't bad multiples, but I would hesitate to put the company in the undervalued category. Even on a forward basis, it's teetering just on the edge between fairly valued and undervalued. Relative to other similar firms, it's also priced at decent levels. As the chart above illustrates, two of the five firms that I compared it to on a price-to-earnings basis are cheaper than it is. Only one of them is cheaper on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. Meanwhile, three of them happen to be cheaper when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach.

Even though I am taking the cautious approach here and rating the company a "Hold," that does not mean that the picture cannot change as new data rolls in. It seems management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year on May 5. Currently, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $713.7 million. Should that come to fruition, it would represent a nice jump over the $665.5 million that the business reported a year earlier. As revenue increases, there is the opportunity for profitability to rise. However, management is likely to report, according to analysts, a decline in earnings per share from $0.66 to $0.65.

That would mean a decline in net profits from $38 million to $37.3 million. The good news is that adjusted earnings per share are forecasted to rise from $0.77 to $0.80. That would mean a slight increase in adjusted net profits from $44.7 million to $45.9 million. If this does improve, it's likely that cash flows will improve as well. But there's no guarantee there.

Takeaway

At its core, I think that Bright Horizons Family Solutions is an intriguing business. I also find it refreshing that management is deliberately closing underperforming locations and achieving success from those efforts. All too often, location-based businesses that decrease location counts have a tendency to see revenue, profits, and cash flows shrink, even if margins improve. But that's not the case here. Management very obviously has something good going on. If only the stock were cheaper, I would be bullish on it. However, for now, I will stick with a "Hold" rating.

data.title: Big Tech Earnings Preview: The AI Supercycle Meets Reality
data.description:
Welcome to Earnings Week

Earnings season has never felt this intense. Five of the Magnificent Seven will report earnings this week, and they'll be playing a game of big-risk poker as much as quarterly reporting. In reality, we've got AI fever worse than ever. As such, the market has bid-up valuations based upon perfect execution and infinite-growth expectations, leaving virtually no margin for error.

Technology stocks are forecasted to grow by 40.9 percent in earnings per share (EPS) in the first quarter of 2026, which is roughly four times faster than the S&P 500’s (SP500) forecasted 12.5 percent EPS growth. Technology stocks are forecasted to continue averaging 41.2 percent EPS growth over the next four quarters — almost two and one-half times faster than the broad market’s forecasted 20.1 percent. Those are some great numbers and those are simply incredible numbers. And they set a very high bar.

On April 29, Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META) will report Q1 results. Apple (AAPL) will follow on April 30 with its fiscal Q2 results. These first 4 companies accounted for roughly $373 billion of the $447 billion spent by the six hyperscalers in fiscal 2025, representing about 83% of reported hyperscaler capex.

The question everyone is trying to answer: when do the enormous investments made by these companies result in substantial profits?

I am confident about the long term prospects for an AI-driven super cycle. The fundamental drivers of this trend are strong, the potential markets addressed by these companies are enormous and these companies have wide economic moats. Short term? Hold onto your seat. We are priced for perfection. It is difficult to achieve perfection on a quarter-by-quarter basis.

In this report, my intention was to provide an overview of what the market expects from each company, along with my personal views on each of these businesses using easy-to-understand language. Next, I have framed the environment as both a bullish and bearish scenario.

My goal is to help readers find it easier to analyze, easier to compare, and most importantly, as helpful as possible when they are looking at the areas that matter most in determining how earnings will affect stock price: the expected performance, key risks associated with the company's performance, upside triggers associated with the company's performance, and how the stock may react based upon their performance.

Microsoft: The Azure Anxiety

Microsoft has a tightrope to walk. The company needs to show that it is getting a return on the significant amount of money it is investing in AI infrastructure, yet continue to show strong growth in Azure. After Microsoft’s most recent earnings report, the stock took a big hit because many investors do not believe that Microsoft’s capital expenditures in AI are going to generate the type of returns that they were expecting. Investors want to see more rapid monetization of Copilot and also demand ongoing strong growth in Azure. If either of these things does not happen quickly then there will be a sell-off in the stock.

Investor Checklist

F3Q revenue guidance is $81.43 billion which represents about 13% year-over-year revenue growth. According to the Bloomberg Consensus, there are expected to be softer trends in each of the business segments. Intelligent Cloud revenue growth is expected to slow sequentially to about 26% year-over-year and Azure is expected to have stable growth of 37-38% year-over-year.

Productivity & Business Processes is expected to have about 11% year-over-year revenue growth with M365 Commercial Cloud expected to grow 13-14% year-over-year. Microsoft 365 Copilot now has 15 million paid seats and daily active user numbers have increased ten times since the product was launched. GitHub Copilot currently has 4.7 million paid subscribers which is an increase of 75% year-over-year. In Q2, Microsoft spent $37.5 billion on capital expenditures with about two-thirds of that spend being on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and Central Processing Units (CPUs).

In the third quarter, Microsoft expects its cloud gross margin to be about 65%, down from where it was last year because of Microsoft’s continued investment in AI.

The Bull/Bear Case

  • Bull Case: Adoption of Microsoft 365 Copilot is happening quicker than what we thought; Azure continues to grow at +37%. Management is confident that operating margins will improve during the back-half of the year because Microsoft’s AI Infrastructure is becoming more productive. I think the Company’s operating and financial profile remain very strong with large amounts of revenue, EBITDA and free cash flow.
  • Bear Case: Azure growth slows down under 35%. Monetization of Copilot falls short of expectations. Or, management gives us some indication that it sees continued margin pressure from AI investments without giving us any insight into when the Company can expect to get a return on those investments. Any indication that the capital expenditure cycle will extend longer than investors expect without seeing any revenue acceleration will be bad news for the stock.

Expected Stock Price Action:

If Microsoft reports a “beat-and-raise” with Azure growing at +38% or better and if Microsoft reports good copilot metrics, I think the stock price could go up anywhere from 5-8%. If Microsoft beats estimates but does not give us much in terms of additional color around their future outlook or if Azure grows at the lower-end of my estimate range, I think you would likely see the stock price fall by about 3-5% as investors start to question whether all the AI spending is ultimately going to pay off. Ultimately, the biggest factor is going to be how management answers questions about their forward-looking guidance. Investors need to see a path to expanding operating margins before they feel comfortable owning shares in Microsoft.

Alphabet: The Search Resilience Test

Alphabet will need to demonstrate that the search division (the largest portion of Alphabet’s total business) can withstand the disruption caused by Generative AI while showing the potential to have a positive impact on its cloud business. In addition, Alphabet must demonstrate that it has the ability to build out sufficient AI-related infrastructure to support the rapidly growing demand from this segment without severely impacting the profit margin. News recently surfaced indicating an expansion of Alphabet’s partnership with Anthropic (ANTHRO) for several additional gigawatts of custom TPU capacity. This signifies a major financial commitment into AI.

Investor Check List

  • Projected total revenue for Q1 '26 is approximately $107.3 billion, and represent a 19.5 percent year over year increase in revenue versus the same period last year. This is greater than the average estimate of analysts (Bloomberg Consensus).
  • Projected Google Cloud revenue for Q1 26 is approximately $19.0 Billion which is a 52.5 percent year over year increase in revenue. The rate of growth accelerated compared to Q4 25. Projected Search revenue for Q1 26 is approximately $59.2 Billion which represents a 14.5 percent increase in revenue when adjusted for changes in foreign currencies. This was slightly better than analyst estimates.
  • Revenue for Q1 26 is projected to be 1.5 percent higher than consensus revenue estimates primarily due to strong results from Google Cloud which contributed positively to both operating income and earnings per share.

Bull and Bear Cases

  • Bull Case: Google Cloud grows by greater than 50 percent, search proves less vulnerable than feared to the disruptions from Generative AI, and there are continued increases in YouTube ad spend. As Google Cloud begins to grow faster, the company will begin to see benefits from operational leverage and management will need to explain how AI investments can be monetized without damaging Search economics.
  • Bear Case: Growth of search slows to below 12 percent because GenAI disrupts user behavior and/or growth of Google Cloud is less than expected despite the company making large investments in the area and/or the operating margins are compressed more than expected because of investments made in AI related infrastructure. If management indicates that GenAI is having a negative impact on high-margin search revenue this would indicate a highly material concern.

Expected Stock Price Action:

My estimate predicts a positive reaction (i.e., a 4-6% increase) if earnings are better than expectations ("beat-and-raise" scenario) while both Cloud's subscription business growth exceeds 50%, and Search maintains a steady rate of growth of 14%. Conversely, my estimate predicts a negative reaction (i.e., an approximately 4-7% decline), if earnings meet expectations but are slightly lower than what was expected ("beat-but-not-raising" scenario). This would occur when either Cloud's subscription business growth falls below 48%, or Search slows down. Investors will begin questioning whether Alphabet will be able to maintain its competitive advantage in the "AI" arena without sacrificing profitability.

Meta: The Efficiency-to-AI Pivot

Meta has a difficult setup in front of it: justify a massive $115-$135 billion capital expenditure budget and continue to grow ad revenue at the same time and show that spending money on artificial intelligence (Advantage +, Llama and infrastructure) is creating real value for the company. Meta just finalized a multi-billion dollar deal to purchase Amazon Graviton5 chips to help meet its growing demand for AI computing that currently exceeds the $135 billion capex budget. Additionally, Meta recently made announcements regarding two partnership deals with Overview Energy and Noon Energy: one focused on developing 1 GW of space-based solar capacity and another focused on developing 100 GWh of storage to support powering their AI computing infrastructure.

Investor check list

  • Estimated first quarter 2026 revenue would be $55.56 billion, according to Seeking Alpha. This represents a 33% year over year increase, which is near the upper limit of guidance provided and slightly above consensus estimates.
  • Management expects total expenses to fall between $162 billion and $169 billion for 2026. Management also believes operating income will grow, as reported in the previous quarterly report. For Freedom Broker, capital expenditures program for 2026 will fall between $115 billion and $135 billion. This estimate exceeds the consensus estimate of $110.7 billion primarily because Meta is strategically expanding compute capacity for Meta superintelligence labs.

Forecasted 2026 revenue growth rate is 24.2%, with an expected operating margin of 34.5%.

Bull/bear case

  • Bull case: Ad revenue will beat estimates due to improvement in Ad Revenue from the use of Advantage+ (AI) with the ability to track users and their behavior through AI Attribution. The company will provide a viable way to monetize the AI investments. The social moat is still intact. The Operating Income continues to grow at a rate greater than the increase in Capital Expenditures, as it appears the Company's efficiency gains are legitimate.
  • Bear case: the deceleration of ad revenue growth. Further increased guidance on capital expenditures, without clear ROI timeframes. Increased Reality Labs' loss expense. If there is an indication that the large investment into AI is not improving ad targeting or creating additional revenue streams; this could indicate poor capital allocation by the company.

Expected investor reaction

If Meta reports a positive earnings report where they meet or exceed their reported first quarter revenue of $56.5 billion and have confidence in the ability to expand operating income despite increased CapEx guidance, I expect a stock price increase of approximately 6-9%. If Meta meets expectations but CapEx guidance falls towards the higher side of their previously reported estimates and management cannot clearly explain how these investments will be monetized, I anticipate a stock price decrease of approximately 5-8%.

Amazon: The AWS Re-Acceleration Story

I just recently changed my rating on Amazon stock from Sell to Buy. Now, Amazon must show that AWS growth is indeed accelerating and that Amazon's advertising business will continue to support growing retail profit margins. Recent announcements indicate that the company is taking Amazon Connect into a set of agentic AI solutions. In addition, the AWS CEO stated he is comfortable paying Microsoft for OpenAI (OPENAI) products.

Everyone is looking at AWS prior to Earnings in anticipation of seeing whether the company’s investments in AI have generated revenue from those expenditures.

Investors Checklist

  • The total revenue projection for 1st Qtr. 26 is around $177.17 Billion.
  • AWS Revenue Growth is expected to accelerate approximately 160 Basis Points Year-Over-Year in 1st Quarter 26. AWS Margins are expected to hit their bottom at 35.2% before modestly increasing.
  • Estimated AWS Revenue Growth for all of 26 is approximately 38% as opposed to consensus estimates of 26%.
  • Advertising revenue forecasts for both 2026 and 2027 have been lowered by an average of 2% from previous estimates due to recent checks that indicate the Advertising Spend YoY growth will slow down in 1st Quarter 26.
  • Estimated capital spending for fiscal 2026 is $205.8 billion.
  • Shipping Costs for Estimated Fiscal Year 26 are estimated to increase by approximately $16 Billion due to increased Oil Prices.

The Bull / Bear Case

  • Bull Case: Amazon's AWS growth will accelerate to 28%+. Advertising revenue will exceed expectations, regardless of what happens with the "headwind" issues. Retail margin expansion will occur. With management affirming its $200 billion capital spending guidance, I expect an upward revision in our estimated free cash flow. Validation of the investment thesis occurs when both AWS re-accelerates and advertising performance strengthens.
  • Bear Case: If AWS grows less than 26% in comparison to where analysts currently predict; if there is a greater-than-expected slowdown in advertising; or if retail margins contract because of higher freight costs resulting from increased oil prices. Evidence that AWS is losing market share to Azure or Google Cloud would be particularly negative for this thesis to either Google Cloud or Microsoft Azure.

Expected investor reaction

If Amazon reports a better-than-expected earnings report with AWS growing 28%+ and reporting strong advertising performance, I anticipate a price increase of 5-7%. However, a better-than-expected report but underwhelming performance in one of those two areas may result in a price decrease of 4-6% as the bull thesis heavily relies on the acceleration of AWS. In fact, nothing else matters to me nearly as much as the rate of growth in AWS.

Apple: The AI-iPhone Upgrade Cycle

Apple has been behind its competitors in terms of AI, however there is great expectation from investors for an "AI iPhone" super-cycle. There have also been reports recently that Apples AI push can create three new hardware categories. As well, incoming CEO John Ternus must prepare the $4 Trillion Behemoth for the AI Era.

Investors Checklist

  • Fiscal Q2 2026 Earnings: the company is on track to meet or exceed expectations of Fiscal Q2 2026 earnings due to its ability to continue to satisfy strong durable demand for iPhones as well as successfully execute its supply chains.
  • Revenue Esimate: $109.73 billion.
  • Global App Store (foreign exchange neutral) YoY Growth Rate: Around 6 percent; Global App Store YoY Growth Rate: approximately 9 percent.
  • New iPhone models are expected in September 2026 and March 2027, while a foldable iPhone could potentially launch in December 2026.

The Bull/Bear Case

  • The Bull case is when Apple has a "beat-and-raise" for iPhone revenue because of a higher-than-anticipated level of consumer interest in its new artificial intelligence (AI) features; also if the company reports acceleration of services revenue greater than 14% and there are signs that the China market is stabilizing. Apple's management team is very confident that the next generation of iPhones which is expected to be launched in late September will begin a multi-year cycle of upgrades.
  • On the other hand, the Bear case is when Apple reports that iPhone revenue was lower than expected. This can happen in one of three ways: either the rate of consumer adoption of AI features is slower than anticipated, the rate of growth of services revenue (which includes iCloud, Apple Music, etc.) slows down from 14%, or China continues to deteriorate. If this happens it could lead to a negative reaction by investors.

Expected Investor Reaction

If Apple "beats" expectations, raises its earnings estimates for future quarters and sees strong sales for its iPhones; then investors should react positively. In fact, Apple could see its stock price increase anywhere from 3 percent to 5 percent. On the other hand, if Apple does better than expectations but doesn't meet their service growth estimate, or sees no improvement in China, investors may sell the stock, resulting in downside pressure. Given that Apple typically trades based on what they say they expect to happen going forward versus how well the most recent quarter performed, comments made during the conference call regarding the expected success of the upcoming iPhone release and consumer interest in AI features will likely have significant impact.

Conclusion: Navigating the Volatility

With regard to the current earning cycle being a test of the AI investment thesis, these five (companies) have invested hundreds of billions of dollars into the belief that AI will transform their business models and provide them with new revenue sources. As such, the market has rewarded that vision with premium valuations. Now it is time to show results.

Below is my framework:

  1. Look at the cloud trio (AWS, Azure & Google Cloud): growth rates of the three are the single most important metrics this week. If all three accelerate that validates the infrastructure build-out for the AI sector. If any of the three meaningfully decelerate - there will be pressure on the entire sector.
  2. Track capex-to-revenue conversion: Investors will only tolerate massive spending if they can see a clear path to Revenue growth. Companies that provide specific AI monetization milestones will outperform those offering vague promises.
  3. Margins matter: acceptable short term compression of margins provided management provides confidence going forward. However, if margins continue to compress without a clearly defined inflection point multiple compression should be expected.
  4. Distinguish Between Beat-and-Raise vs. Beat-and-hold: while beating the quarter is not enough in this environment, guidance raises indicate confidence. Guidance holds indicate caution, the market will punish even strong quarters based on the latter.
  5. Have conviction in your long-term thesis: short term Volatility is inevitable when you're priced for "perfection." However, the long-term super cycle for AI remains intact - use any weakness from beat-and-hold scenarios as an opportunity to add positions in high quality names.

The technology sector is expected to be at the top of earnings growth in 2026 with an estimated 45 percent year-over-year growth rate. This increase is due to growing contributions to profitability from artificial intelligence.

I am bullish on the long-term fundamentals of big tech, however, this week may bring some obstacles. Large swings should be expected, and investors should remain disciplined, remember that in a priced-for-perfection market even great results may disappoint if they are not exceptional.

data.title: Clorox: Reduced Complexity Paves The Way For Multiple Expansion
data.description:
Clorox (CLX) has been underperforming on a YTD basis, and in the last year alone, it is down nearly 29%. This poor performance somewhat misses the mark on improving fundamentals and there could be an opportunity to own the shares here, post-FQ3 earnings. With a 5% dividend yield, the stock screens rather attractively.

Business Profile

Clorox makes branded cleaning and household products under brands like its namesake Clorox, Pine-Sol, and Glad. The company is also the owner of a wide variety of other brands like Kingsford, best known for charcoal grilling; Burt's Bees, best known for lip and skin care; and Hidden Valley Ranch. The company operates in nearly 100+ countries around the world and has a leading position in at least 80% of the categories it operates in.

Clorox has four key segments - Health & Wellness, Household, Lifestyle, and International. By revenue mix, Health & Wellness is approximately 38%, Household is 28%, Lifestyle is 18%, and International is 15%.

Within this, it's worth noting that Clorox actually gives us a bit more detail on the product mixes that they sell. The company has disclosed that cleaning products are 44% of the business, bags and wraps are 15%, food products are 12%, and cat litter is 10%.

It's worth noting the customer concentration for Clorox. The company has Walmart (WMT) as its largest customer at 27% of sales. No other customer accounts for more than 10% of sales, but the five largest customers for the company account for nearly half of the company's net sales in the last three fiscal years. So, it is very heavily dependent on a few select retailers globally. The company is largely domestic with only a small international exposure, approximately 85% domestic.

The company also just recently purchased GOJO Industries, which makes Purell. The company paid $2.25 billion in cash. This included certain tax benefits of $330 million, so the net purchase price was $1.92 billion. Purell does $800 million in annual sales and has done 5% sales growth over the last 5 years. Clorox has a long-term algorithm for 3-5% sales growth, so this is accretive to the top line. The company noted in the deal closure press release that it will be accretive to EBITDA and EPS in the second year, not the first year. Synergies will total approximately $50 million.

This is the company's first major acquisition in several years, with most other brands in the portfolio having been acquired pre-COVID.

Source: Investor Presentation

Clorox is in an interesting vertical within consumer staples because, for the tracked channels it operates in, the company has seen private label competition share stagnate at around 17%, rather than increase. This is a much more favorable profile than other verticals.

Source: Investor Presentation

Financial Profile & Fiscal Q3 Earnings Setup

With the recent M&A news, the transaction is largely an FY27 consideration from a full earnings standpoint. With that being said, it's worth considering the state of the balance sheet. As of last quarter, the company had $2.56 billion in net debt ($227 million in cash against total debt of $2.79 billion).

If we consider the adjusted operating income profile in FY26 to be approximately $1.0 billion (which assumes they hit the midpoint of guidance), then leverage on a net debt/adjusted EBITDA basis is approximately 2.1x. After the financing for Gojo begins, net debt will increase to about $4.5 billion, and that assumes the tax benefits have reduced the purchase price like the acquisition release mentions. Then, we have to consider that the transaction adds about $160 million in EBITDA (given the purchase price at 11.9x) and look out to fiscal '27 when it will be formally included in total Clorox operations. Adj. EBITDA then will be just shy of $1.6 billion, and net leverage will be 2.8x.

So, it's not the most comfortable balance sheet in consumer staples, but there are two important things to consider. First, Purell is a high quality brand with high awareness and a category leading position. Second, the company produces enough free cash flow in a year (approximately ~$770 million in FY25 alone) where they can delever close to three quarters of a turn, if not a full turn post-Gojo, to where the balance sheet can be in check under 2x net leverage by FY28. This staves away leverage concerns.

With that being said, the company is coming off of a great FY25 where they had 7% organic volume growth and 5% organic sales growth. This was largely driven by incremental shipments that happened following a recovery from both their cyberattack and ERP transition, as there were shelf space disruptions as a result of these factors. The ERP tailwind was worth about 3.5-4pts of the 5% OSG% growth.

As we approach Q3 earnings this week, the setup seems relatively straight forward.

Recall the guide:

  • Net sales -6% to -10%
    • <1 point impact from VMS divestiture
    • OSG% -5% to -9%
    • 7.5% impact from reversal of the impact of incremental shipments associated with the prior year
  • GM% down 50-100 bps
  • SG&A 16% of sales
    • 90 bps of impact from strategic investments
  • Advertising 11% of sales
  • Tax rate 24%
  • Adj. EPS $5.95-$6.30 (down 18-23% y/y)
    • Excludes 35 cents from strategic investments; includes 90 cents from reversal of the impact from incremental shipments associated with ERP

The company is lapping a net sales figure of -8% y/y and an organic sales figure of -2% y/y.

The -8% bridge to -2% is primarily due to the VMS and Argentina business divestiture, so this doesn't need to be reconciled. The -2% organic sales figure was primarily driven by unfavorable price mix, rather than one-time factors like ERP.

I am modeling organic sales growth of 2% but reported growth of 1%, gross margin contraction of 50 bps, and SG&A at 16% flat with an 11% advertising rate, normal below the line items, including a 24% tax rate. This equates to $1.65 in adj. EPS vs. street at $1.54. This should set up a positive reaction for shares.

Valuation & FY27 Estimates

What we have here with CLX is a normalization. The cyber attack and the ERP change are moving behind the company after FY26, as is the incremental shelf restocking. This is setting a clean base of financials for next year, so I don't believe it's appropriate to value CLX on a fiscal 2026 basis.

The simplest way to understand the earnings potential for next year on CLX is to assume that they hit the midpoint of their Adj. EPS guidance this year, which would equate to $6.13.

Recall that the company has a financial algo in place:

  • Sales growth of 3-5%
  • Adj. EBIT Margin expansion of +25-50bps
  • FCF of 11-13%

If we assume FY27 is on-algo, then we get $7.4 billion in total sales and adjusted operating income of $1.2 billion.

After factoring in interest expense of about $90 million and a tax rate of 24%, we arrive at a net income of $6.79. This would be a growth of approximately 10.8%, assuming the midpoint of guidance this year is hit. I am also assuming that the other income/expense line item before pre-tax earnings is negligible. There is no clear pattern for forecasting it.

Taking a step back, that would put CLX on an FY27 EPS of 14.5x. That is relatively cheap for double-digit earnings growth and 4% top line growth. There would even be scope for multiple expansions to the 17-18x area. That would be a $120 stock for 20% upside from the current level. This also assumes no accretion from Gojo next year.

Additionally, the dividend yield of 5% is certainly starting to pop up on screeners and can add to the total return profile. There are not many large-cap consumer staples names with a 5% yield.

Versus peers, valuation is as follows:

  • Procter & Gamble (PG) - trades at 21.6x forward earnings
  • Kimberly-Clark (KMB) - trades at 13.8x forward earnings
  • Church & Dwight (CHD) - trades at 25.4x forward earnings
  • Colgate (CL) - trades at 22.1x forward earnings

Clorox is getting more of a Kimberly-Clark multiple, despite about ~2 points better top line growth on a forward basis and while the earnings growth rate is similar in the low-double-digit region, investors don't have to stomach a major acquisition like that of Kenvue (KVUE). There is clear room for the Clorox multiple to expand.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

Investing with Clorox comes with several risks. First, the company was the victim of a major cyber attack in 2023. This led to operational disruptions and cost the company over $350 million. While cyber attacks are rare for major public corporations, they can happen.

Largely the biggest risk for Clorox is declining volume growth with the persistent threat of private label. As private label products are often substantially cheaper than branded versions for very similar levels of quality, the company's volume gains will need to primarily come through product innovation and trustworthiness from the consumer.

That brings about a third risk in that consumer spending has to be viable enough to drive spending on branded goods. Low consumer confidence or spending ability will disable volume growth for Clorox. The company itself earlier this year even noted that 88% of consumers are exhibiting value-seeking behaviors.

Conclusion

Clorox, at face value, doesn't have a lot going for it in 2026. It is the normalization process of an outsized 2025 as the company recovered from a major cyberattack. With all of the movements and adjustments about to be behind them, the company is entering the back half of the fiscal year with momentum. As we look out to fiscal 2027, the profile of mid-single-digit top-line growth and double-digit earnings growth at an attractive valuation with a 5% dividend yield is very enticing and worth a look at from investors today.

data.title: Mastercard: The Market Is Pricing In Fear, But My Bull Case Points To $590
data.description:
Introduction

So far, this year has not been favorable to Financials, which is something that I find quite amusing since I view this as one of the most clearly identifiable beneficiaries of AI. As a result, guess what I have been buying of late, apart from Amazon (AMZN)? Stocks such as Mastercard (MA) and S&P Global (SPGI) are positions that I have been actively expanding in my portfolios. But I also think their sparring partners Visa (V) and Moody's (MCO) are great picks, as I explained in these two head-to-head comparisons:

  1. Moody's Vs. S&P Global
  2. Visa Vs. Mastercard

In a few days, Mastercard will report its Q1 2026 earnings; shortly after, Visa will have reported. There are a few things I think need to be considered before the print, and that is why I am writing this article.

Mastercard At A Glance

I bet everyone who is reading this article knows what Mastercard is. Allow me to spend a few words, however, on how I view it. Some of my readers know I particularly like infrastructure plays. If they are similar to toll booths, that is even better. These kinds of companies usually need to deploy billions in CapEx. After all, infrastructure is expensive to maintain. But then, there are digital infrastructure companies that combine the unique architectural role of infrastructure with the leaner financial structure of digital businesses. Mastercard is exactly one of these companies, as it owns one of the most important rails through which digital money flows around the world. By nature, it is a volume-driven business. Since digital payments are still not the number one choice in most countries around the world, Mastercard has a long-lasting opportunity to grow volumes. In addition to charging fees on gross dollar volume, switched transactions, and cross-border volume, it also charges a fee on currency conversion. However, the more it has developed its payment business, the more Mastercard has gathered data and developed a deep know-how on fraud detection, cybersecurity, tokenization, business insights, and similar. This has led to the creation of the value-added services and solutions business (VAS) that distinguishes Mastercard from its larger competitor, Visa. In case you don't know, roughly 60% of Mastercard's value-added services are network-linked, and this carries a big meaning with it because it shows the percentage of VAS revenues that grow organically with transaction volume growth. The advantage is clear: to sell its VAS, Mastercard doesn't truly need a separate sales cycle.

Mastercard's Key Economic Drivers

I wrote the paragraph above at the cost of sounding repetitive to many. But this is the direct ferry that carries us to the other side of the river, where we can deal with the main economic drivers that impact Mastercard. Consumer spending is the first, and in this situation, inflation is not a headwind as long as it doesn't truly affect consumption rates. This was recently discussed at the Wolfe Research FinTech forum, where, once again, we heard confirmation about healthy consumer spending. Secondly, cross-border travel is extremely important, as well as e-commerce volume. The digitalization of payments represents a tailwind that can act as a factor that partially offsets the cyclical downturns in spending, thanks to the cash-to-card conversion trend. Finally, FX also plays a role.

One of the reasons why payment rail giants such as Visa and Mastercard are underperforming this year is linked to stablecoins. I already explained how I view stablecoins as a tailwind, especially in Mastercard's case, which was further supported by Mastercard's Chief Commercial Payments Officer Raj Seshadri at the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026, when she explained Mastercard's strategy in becoming the interoperability layer.

The recently announced BVNK acquisition, a stablecoin payments orchestrator, shows Mastercard's commitment to becoming a key player in this space. The acquisition makes sense because building from scratch a similar orchestrator internally would have taken years. Since stablecoins are expected to become real payment rails, even though they are not replacing cards, Mastercard wants to be present as the interoperability layer that sits between fiat money and tokenized money. It is basically what MA already does in the cards business: it acts as the bridge that connects different ecosystems by creating a trustworthy infrastructure with set rules, compliance standards, and a global reach. MA can monetize its stablecoin business in four ways: spread on send/convert/receive flows, FX, value-added services (MA's true cash cow), and wallet-as-a-service.

This acquisition is important because, traditionally, stablecoins are useful in high-friction flows, where MA was weak and, thus, undermonetized the opportunity. In addition, those who fear that MA will suffer from disintermediation can now see that MA is strengthening itself by integrating with those payment rails that should have been a threat. We have often heard MA say that it is rail-agnostic. Here we have further evidence of this.

Mastercard's Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

Last quarter, Mastercard guided for Q1 2026 net revenue growth of around 10%, which means that the implied revenue could be $8.3B. OpEx should be up 9% YoY, thus helping Mastercard's margin expansion trajectory. However, we also know that Q1 2026 will have a $200M one-time restructuring charge, which will have an impact on the company's EPS. Moreover, during the Q4 call, we were informed that January was a strong month with switched volume up 9% and cross-border up 13% YoY. However, things changed in March, and this leaves the biggest question mark over Mastercard's earnings. With the Middle East basically closed, I expect to see lower cross-border volume than previously anticipated. Therefore, I think that the role of VAS will be even more important. It is true that 60% is linked to volume growth, but if we see it consistently grow at a pace between 15% and 18%, then we would see the real structural strength of this new segment. If, on the other hand, the print reports VAS' growth below 15%, it will be a problem

What will matter even more will be the FY26 guidance. Currently, it targets a revenue growth in the high single digits. With Q2 on its way and Q3 coming, the two strongest cross-border quarters are going to be extremely important, and we will have to listen to what the management has to say in this regard. Interestingly, analysts are bullish, and we see that in the last three months, we have 16 EPS upward revisions versus only two downward revisions. However, 12 out of 17 analysts moderated their revenue revisions.

In a scenario where VAS reports revenue growth of 19% and cross-border volume grows at 12% (my base-case scenario), we could be expecting Q1 adj. EPS of $4.45 (the consensus is $4.41), which could lead to FY26 EPS of $20.00. In this case, applying a fwd earnings multiple of 25 gives us a price target of $500, which is, more or less, the price Mastercard trades at today. This would mean that the stock is more or less fairly priced. I want to be honest, though. When I say that I am buying Mastercard, it is because I am buying it for the bull case, where the main assumptions are cross-border volume growth of 14% and VAS growth of 23%. Even though Q1 and Q2 might see slower cross-border volume, I think we could be in accumulation territory because, once the Iran war ends, we could see a traffic rebound that will create a favorable setup for Mastercard. Here, I am using a 28x multiple, which, multiplied by the FY26 EPS of $21.00, gives me a target price close to $590, which is my current target.

As a result, I think Mastercard remains a very interesting buy ahead of earnings, with the addition that, in case of a dip after the report is out, it would become even more compelling.

data.title: Lowe's: Approaching Fair Value Amid Housing Weakness (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW) have been just a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 9%. While the company has managed tariff headwinds fairly well, sales have been limited by a muted construction spending environment, both in terms of new construction as well as renovation and repair activity. At the same time, we have seen LOW be increasingly aggressive on the M&A front to catch up to Home Depot (HD) among “pro” customers. I last covered Lowe’s in January, when I reiterated the stock a “Sell,” and that call has played out with shares down 13% since then. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit Lowe’s.

Starting with the macro backdrop, while there were hopes of a recovery in the housing market in 2026, this looks increasingly unlikely to occur. After briefly dipping below 6%, mortgage rates have moved back up, with the upside risk to inflation likely postponing any Fed rate cuts. We will likely need to see 10-year yields sustainably below 4% to get mortgage rates below 6%, and that appears unlikely. Sure enough, existing home sales fell to a nine-month low in March. The level of housing transactions is deeply depressed at about 4 million units.

Many existing homeowners with low-rate mortgages are hesitant to move and take on a new mortgage while challenged affordability limits the pool of potential buyers. Plus, many post-2022 buyers have very limited home equity, making moving more difficult. Transaction levels are important for Lowe’s business as homeowners often do some work repairing homes to get them ready for sale while buyers may also take on some projects (from repainting a room to remodeling a bathroom) after buying the house. Fewer transactions limit this amount of activity. I believe existing home sales are basically “as bad as it gets,” but a recovery is unlikely until 2027.

With this channel closed, Lowe’s is dependent on existing homeowners undertaking projects on their own homes. Typically, these projects are more likely to occur during a period of home price appreciation, which is not the case in much of the nation, or low rates. Plus, while there are inevitable repair jobs that arise, many of these projects are discretionary and can be postponed when consumers feel less confident about the future. That appears to be happening.

Indeed, Lowe’s has seen ongoing weakness in do-it-yourself (“DIY”) activity, which is the company’s primary focus even as M&A has pushed it further into the Pro segment. Many renovation projects are discretionary. With interest rates elevated and affordability challenges front and center, many consumers are simply delaying big-ticket purchases and projects. With the affordability backdrop only having worsened over the past three months given the move higher in oil prices, I struggle to see this momentum shifting in 2026, especially with the spring/early summer so critical for construction-related activity.

Plus, its acquisitions of FBM and ADG have increased Lowe’s exposure to housing construction. As you can see below, the number of homes currently under construction has fallen for nearly four straight years, a prolonged recession in housing activity. Still, actual construction levels are still above pre-COVID norms. This speaks to the magnitude of the boom post-COVID when rates plunged and prices surged. Because of the level of new construction, many Sun Belt markets are still struggling with excess supply, weighing on prices. I expect Sun Belt pricing to remain negative this year, though we are seeing signs of some stabilization, and construction levels are unlikely to bottom until late 2026 in my view, remaining an ongoing drag on Lowe’s results.

The company last reported earnings on February 25, when Lowe’s reported a Q4 profit of $1.98, which beat estimates by $0.04. Same-store sales were up 1.3% as its aggressive investments in the Pro channel are helping to offset some of the pressures we have seen. Q4 was also when we saw rates decline and was before the start of the Iran War; as such, I am cautious that trends have worsened a bit since then. Gross margins declined 40 bps to 32.3%, and while tariff pressures should fade, I expect margins to remain muted in 2026 given lackluster consumer demand.

Additionally, Lowe’s ended the year with 3.3x debt/EBITDA. This was down 0.1x from Q3, and leverage should steadily improve as it uses free cash flow to pay down debt. LOW has a 2.75x leverage target, and I expect this to be achieved by Q3 2027, barring incremental M&A. With debt so far above target, I expect buybacks to be on hold for all of this year. Now, shares offer a 2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure given Lowe’s cash flow.

Alongside results, the company offered a fairly downbeat outlook for the year. It expects sales to be up 7%-9%, aided by M&A, but EPS will be just $12.25-$12.75 as operating margins are expected to be just about 11.7%. Back in January, I was targeting $12.75-$12.90 in 2026 EPS, which was below consensus at the time. Lowe’s is offering an outlook even below that.

Even after its recent underperformance, Lowe’s shares continue to trade at a premium valuation; Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener rates shares a “D-.” As you can see below, the stock trades at a premium to the sector as well as to its own 5-year history, though not as pronounced of one as a few months ago. The market is still pricing in a fairly optimistic increase in housing-related spending this year, which I am cautious about. I also struggle with Lowe’s trading at a premium to itself given its over-leveraged balance sheet and paused share buybacks. While valuation alone may not be sufficient to drive a “Sell” rating, it precludes rushing to buy shares in my view.

Now, I do not see Lowe’s results getting worse in 2026 relative to 2025. Indeed, even if we saw a more meaningful decline in economic activity, I believe the downside in LOW’s earnings is fairly modest. We have basically endured a four-year housing recession, and in many areas, the level of activity is approaching levels that are as low as they can get. Metrics like existing home sales may not recover, but I struggle to see them falling much further.

Essentially, I see Lowe’s benefiting from a very tepid improvement in results, in part due to the integration of M&A and scaling up of its Pro offerings. I am looking for 2026 earnings of $12.40-$12.85, reflecting some deferral of DIY projects given inflation pressures and new construction levels that stabilize by late this year. That leaves shares just under 19x. Given the cyclicality of its business and the weakness of its balance sheet, I have felt 18x is a fair multiple, or $225-$230. This is about 5% below current levels, or ~3% including its dividend.

Frankly, I do not view a 3% downside as sufficient to merit an ongoing “Sell,” recognizing that no forecast is perfect. Shares are now close enough to fair value after their recent decline to be a “Hold” as the market has sufficiently deflated expectations around a housing recovery. That said, I would definitely not rush to buy LOW and would look elsewhere in the market for better opportunities. I would want to see shares pull back below $210 before there is enough upside to buy, given ongoing weakness in housing fundamentals, but after this drop, there is no pressing need to sell.
